item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Obviously 2+2 is not 4. 2+2 is actually θ.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cuil Theory has taken me down a rabbit hole and I have no regrets.
Placeholder, tell me, why are all of your works so confusing and amazing? ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A general note: If you go into an SCP article expecting a watered-down concept that's easily digestible for the sake of maximizing intrigue, you've definitely done something wrong. The Foundation has to deal with and document all anomalies somehow, no matter how unconventional they might be. If you don't like complexity, you shouldn't take interest in anomalies that affect the congruencies of the principle of abstraction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was an enjoyable read. Very well written, especially for being your first skip (something I’m still attempting to get around it). I like its sense of mystery and think it fits very well with 4000. Its very original yet still reminds me of 2521, toaster, and the former library.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i LOVE 4000 riffs, and i love bureaucratohazards, and i love this.

i think it's time 4000 got its own tag considering the mini-canon that's developed from people developing stories from it.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

It's /r/SUBREDDITNAME, SCP version!

It's an interesting concept, but uncharacteristically for me, not one that I found enjoyable - almost like it goes so far into asserting its particular quirk that it forgets to be an enjoyable read. The generic terms used throughout sort of forced my mind to gloss over the text (which is an impressive achievement, honestly.)

That's fine, but what got my -1 was the "creepy note at the end", which appears to exist without justification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent, while I don't fully understand it, I did see an explanation that made sense, linking to SCP-4000 is always fun, as it's one of my favorite articles.

+1

item.title: Re: Not my cup 'o tea, but it's well done
item.content:encoded: What a kind and respectful comment. This was a blast to write, and I'm excited to do more. Thank you very much.

item.title: Not my cup 'o tea, but it's well done
item.content:encoded: As a longtime reader, I try not to entirely base my votes on whether or not I necessarily like a concept. As is the case here. I couldn't quite wrap my head around this one, but I wasn't a huge fan of Taboo either. That being said, I can absolutely tell that you put a lot of yourself into this, and for your first skip, this is incredibly ambitious. +1 for that and I do look forward to seeing more from you!

item.title: Re: Upvote no.101
item.content:encoded: Oh lol was looking at the overall rating haha

item.title: Re: Upvote no.101
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much for your upvote and crit! And, for the record, you're upvote no. 133 :D

item.title: Upvote no.101
item.content:encoded:
I quite like this, although I can definately understand why it isn't everyone's cup of tea. I do like the format screw, but I think it has been slightly over done here as the story was a bit hard to follow. This kind of skip requires the reader to spend some time deciphering the text which, understandbly, isn't liked by everyone.

Overall, I think this was cleverly done and fun to read, even though the sheer volume of blue and green text hurt my eyeballs. Also liked the crosslinking to the woods that cannot be named.

+1, as the headache I got from reading this wasn't that bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’ve never been a fan of these DoMC skips. Most of the time it seems like an excuse to use some format screw to make your SCP unique. I don’t mind it if I haven’t seen it before. However, this kind of thing is as old as the hills.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The color coding is intended to give the article a more clear link to SCP-4000, whose mythology it builds upon.

That much is obvious, but 4000 uses the color-coding more sparingly, and with less variety than what's presented here. If you take a given section of 4000, you're likely to find only a few examples of colored text, relatively spread-out, and usually only one color at a time.

The way that same coloring is implemented in this article only accomplishes a visual assault.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea here is unique and presents some interesting opportunities, but the execution fails to expand on those and do anything notable with the concept. The interview log and the poem don't constitute an interesting story to me; for all intents and purposes, I see this as an article without any real story to it. So much time is spent just explaining the anomaly and the necessary context to understand it that the end result feels flat and lacks any traditionally engaging elements that would give me reason to care about it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback. The color coding is intended to give the article a more clear link to SCP-4000, whose mythology it builds upon. I will keep your criticism in mind in future articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I first saw this, I couldn't exactly put my feelings into words without coming off as abrasive/aggressive so… yes. What Nat said. From my perspective the visual layout of the article seems intent on getting me to not read it, with the color coding and the text blocks and the opening monologue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I don't enjoy reading a story then I'm not going to finish it.

You're also misinterpreting what I meant by "mindnumbingly." Nothing about this article "insulted" me; it was just boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I apologize that my article was not an enjoyable experience for you. Thank you for your feedback. I would have appreciated it if you finished the rest of the article, as many others have done so and thoroughly enjoyed it, but I understand how you feel and I am sorry that my work insulted your intelligence. If you're interested, which I know you likely aren't, there is a declass on this article at the following link:

[https://www.reddit.com/r/SCPDeclassified/comments/hjifwh/file_5242_scpinteger_clarification/]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so mindnumbingly dense that my eyes glossed over because I got to the halfway point. There is absolutely nothing here that makes me want to dissect the densities and actually care about what is happening. It's just a prosaic block.

-1.

Also, a general note: If your article opens by giving a tedious description of a concept then you're probably something wrong.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: I'll see what I can do :)
more bureaucratohazards, please

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much!

All icons and images shown were created by Calibri Bold.

This SCP is so cool!!
I'm sorry if I sound picky.Did you create the image used in this SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much for your time and your feedback. It is unfortunate that my article was not an enjoyable experience for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the hell did I just read? I did not enjoy reading this, and it didn't even feel like there was any motivation to figure it out. Some SCPs are confusing but draw you in so you learn more about it. This one… well it's like if you gave me a puzzle to solve but told me the reward for solving it was a rock thrown at my head. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your time, your flattering comment and your upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just think this is terrific. Originality is still alive and well in the SCP Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excited to see that you've posted this! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much for your time, your comment, your upvote, and your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gave this piece a +1 for ambition and style although there are no small number of logical inconsistencies within its framework. MAF pointed out many of them and because of that I do not want to deeply rehash what has been done here by you, author. But I do want to make sure you know that this is a promising piece.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much for your crit, your time, your comment, and your upvote!
I'm very proud to have critted this. Good work, and I look forward to what else you produce.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I think I see what you're saying. I'm waiting on a response from PeppersGhost to get their take on this definition of bureaucratohazard, so I'll keep your thoughts and his in mind when I do a rewrite of that disclaimer. Thank you for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But in that description, you've basically stated the core flaw — it's often in ways that don't mesh correctly. If it was a perfect, undetectable restructuring of core concepts, I could appreciate that being given its own terminology; hell, at that point I'd agree that 'anomaly' is maybe an invalid term. But a flawed version of the same system, to me at least, doesn't seem to be different to any other given anomaly. If it's possible to detect this bureaucratohazard, it means by definition that there must be an alternative normality with which to compare it, which means that it can still be classified as abnormal, and is therefore just an anomaly with a fancy new name. If there wasn't an alternative normality then I could see your point, but that's clearly not what's happening here.

A little further down in that same memo is the line

even if anomalies are irrational, they are factual

…which should help to outline my problem here. A bureaucratohazard is still an anomaly. And so are any other anomalies which conflate the real with the abstract, and the physical with the metaphysical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your time and feedback. I try to keep my definition consistent with the example laid out in Sheldon Katz's memo in SCP-4703. There, PeppersGhost mentions:

"Generally we interpret and exercise the law through the scrutiny of semantics, reasoning, and precedent, yet bureaucratohazards such as SCP-4703 are not necessarily predicated on such things—in fact, the law as most know it has very little to do with the matter."

Anomalies, from my understanding, are defined as being inconsistent with baseline reality, the set of all universal principles considered 'normal'. Bureaucratohazards adjust baseline reality around themselves to include them- often, in ways that don't quite mesh correctly, which allows the Foundation to discover them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of an anomaly is inconsistent here. All anomalies are things that the universe says is true, even thought it shouldn't — the universe says that a particular statue can move, or there's a number between █ and █, or that SCP-055 is a self-keeping secret. The whole point of the Foundation is that these things exist despite contradicting what we formerly presumed to be true.

Such things are not anomalous, as they are a fact of baseline reality; they always have been, and always will be, true

And this isn't what "anomalous" means. "Anomalous" means that it's something that contradicts what we think is true, but continues to exist regardless. I really, really don't like the redefinition of bureaucratohazard — an anomalous facet of law — to encompass pretty much everything anomalous, particularly when you go out of your way to wax philosophical about how actually, no, this one is special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much for your time, your comment, and your upvote. I've been back and forth with the coloration, several people have argued both sides, but I think it's a more clear visual tie to Taboo and those woods that suck to talk about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read and enjoyed this one while it was still in the draft process, though I see there's been quite a few changes since then. To be super nitpicky, I feel like the interview was a little too much, though to be fair it did get the point of the SCP across nicely. Also, honestly, the colored words in the text are really grating to look at for me. +1 regardless, but I'd be more confident in the vote if the article was easier to look at on that front. That aside, I absolutely adore this concept and I think this is a really solid first skip.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - OPEN
item.content:encoded: It really is best to just leave it to staff. Everything will get tagged eventually.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much! There's an internal logic behind it that's purposefully obscured. This SCP is intended to feel like a logic puzzle.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - OPEN
item.content:encoded: Ah, my apologies. Thank you, I'll leave it to the staff.

item.title: Staff Post - OPEN
item.content:encoded: Hey Placeholder McD: I've removed the tags you added as only one was a valid tag. If you aren't familiar with the tagging system it's best to leave it to staff.
I have very little idea what the frick-frack-cracker-jack I just read, but that doesn't change the fact it's amazingly weird.

item.title: Thank you for reading my skip!
item.content:encoded:
fanart?
I guess...

credits to popsioak for this abomination

This is my first skip, and I put a lot of heart and soul into it. It went through a lot of edits and rewrites. Thanks to everyone on the SCPD Discord, IRC, and Idea Crit who gave feedback; they were all super crucial in helping me focus my idea and develop a narrative.

Special thanks to PeppersGhost for writing such good skips and laying out the concepts that I built on in this article. 4000 and 4703 are fantastic and I hope my contribution to this larger story is worthy!

EDIT: Extremely special thanks to Calibri Bold for designing and coding the custom ACS icons!

+ bonus explanation after you've read BOTH addenda:
- bonus explanation after you've read BOTH addenda (and the above skips):

A Foundation researcher did some bureaucratohazardous anti-math to derive the existence of LOGICIAN, an antilogical abstract entity that rules over the forest that's so damn hard to talk about (and is worshipped by the Fae). He said LOGICIAN's name, not aware of its ties to name-related phenomena, and subsequently took on a bit of its identity. However, it's sheer abstractness and unreason caused his own identity to become abstracted by LOGICIAN, as a PUNISHMENT for trying to speak about it. Thus, reality was adjusted so that he was cursed with the name Dr. Placeholder. Anyone else who attempts to communicate about it meets a similar fate. The LOGICIAN is the bureaucratohazard complex, but only those who understand that it's really an entity are forced to live with that knowledge, as Dr. Placeholder was.

more on cuil theory
you've been warned

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nfdEdE96En0

edits:

  • added Calibri Bold's abstracted ACS Icons
  • added the VOLATILITY image in the article
  • converted to DoMC format for consistency
